metadata.title: Top 100 Java Interview Questions
metadata.description: Most probable Java interview questions compiled from top Java learning sources. Each question is tagged with the sources it appears in and a frequency ranking based on how often it shows up across those sources.

JavaTpoint.strength: Massive Q&A bank, well-organized by subtopic (core, collections, multithreading, JVM, JDBC)

Baeldung.strength: Deep, high-quality articles; great for explain-this-concept style questions

InterviewBit.strength: Structured difficulty progression from basic to advanced

GeeksforGeeks.strength: Broad coverage across every Java subtopic

Journaldev.strength: Strong on collections, concurrency, and Spring

Programiz.strength: Beginner-friendly explanations adaptable into interview answers

frequency_legend.MOST_FREQ: Asked in almost every Java interview. Appears in 5-6 of the 6 sources. Master these first.
frequency_legend.MED: Asked in many interviews. Appears in 3-4 sources. Expected knowledge for 2+ years experience.
frequency_legend.LOW: Appears in 1-2 sources or is a deep-dive follow-up. Expected for senior roles or specialized interviews.

questions.question: What is Java?
questions.answer: A high-level, object-oriented, platform-independent, multithreaded, secure, robust programming language developed by James Gosling at Sun Microsystems in 1991 (released 1995). Its 'write once, run anywhere' promise comes from bytecode + JVM.

questions.question: Why is Java platform-independent?
questions.answer: The javac compiler produces bytecode (.class), not machine code. Any machine with a compatible JVM can execute this bytecode, so the same .class file runs on Windows, Linux, and Mac without modification.

questions.question: Difference between JDK, JRE, and JVM?
questions.answer: JVM is the virtual machine that runs bytecode. JRE is JVM + standard libraries (enough to run Java apps). JDK is JRE + compilers + tools like javac, javadoc, jdb (needed to develop).

questions.question: Is Java purely object-oriented?
questions.answer: No. Java has primitive types (int, char, boolean, etc.) that are not objects. A purely OO language treats everything as an object; Java does not.

questions.question: What is bytecode?
questions.answer: An intermediate, platform-neutral instruction set produced by the Java compiler. The JVM either interprets it or JIT-compiles it to native code at runtime.

questions.question: What is the JIT compiler?
questions.answer: Just-In-Time compiler, part of the JVM. It compiles frequently executed bytecode ('hot spots') into native machine code at runtime to boost performance.

questions.question: What is a ClassLoader?
questions.answer: A JVM subsystem that loads class files into memory on demand. The hierarchy is Bootstrap → Extension (Platform) → Application (System) loaders, following the parent-delegation model.

questions.question: What are the main memory areas managed by JVM?
questions.answer: Heap (objects), Stack (method frames, local variables), Method Area / Metaspace (class metadata), PC Register (current instruction), and Native Method Stack.

questions.question: Stack vs Heap memory?
questions.answer: Stack stores method calls and local primitives/references; it's thread-local and fast. Heap stores all objects and arrays; shared across threads and managed by the garbage collector.

questions.question: Difference between == and .equals()?
questions.answer: == compares references (same object in memory). .equals() compares content/value as defined by the class. String and wrapper classes override equals; Object.equals() by default behaves like ==.

questions.question: What are the main features of Java?
questions.answer: Simple, object-oriented, platform-independent, secure, robust, architecture-neutral, portable, multithreaded, distributed, dynamic, and high-performance (via JIT).

questions.question: What is the default value of a local variable?
questions.answer: There is none. Local variables must be explicitly initialized before use (compiler-enforced). Instance and class variables do get defaults: 0 for numeric, false for boolean, null for references.

questions.question: Can we execute a Java program without main()?
questions.answer: Not in modern Java. Historically a static block could print output before main was called, but the JVM still required main as the entry point. Since Java 7 it's strictly required.

questions.question: Difference between final, finally, and finalize()?
questions.answer: final is a keyword (constant variable / non-overridable method / non-extendable class). finally is a block that always executes after try/catch for cleanup. finalize() was a method called by the GC before reclaiming an object; deprecated since Java 9.

questions.question: What is a package in Java?
questions.answer: A namespace that groups related classes/interfaces. Benefits: avoids name clashes, provides access control, makes code easier to locate and maintain.

questions.question: What are the four pillars of OOP?
questions.answer: Encapsulation, Inheritance, Polymorphism, and Abstraction.

questions.question: What is encapsulation?
questions.answer: Bundling data (fields) and behavior (methods) inside a class, and restricting direct access to data via private fields with public getters/setters.

questions.question: What is inheritance?
questions.answer: A mechanism where a subclass acquires the properties and methods of a superclass via the extends keyword. Java supports single inheritance for classes and multiple inheritance via interfaces.

questions.question: Why doesn't Java support multiple inheritance with classes?
questions.answer: To avoid the diamond problem (ambiguity when two parents have the same method). Java solves this by letting a class extend only one class but implement multiple interfaces.

questions.question: What is polymorphism?
questions.answer: 'One interface, many implementations.' Two kinds: compile-time (method overloading) and runtime (method overriding).

questions.question: Difference between method overloading and overriding?
questions.answer: Overloading: same method name, different parameter lists, same class; resolved at compile time. Overriding: subclass redefines a parent method with the same signature; resolved at runtime via dynamic dispatch.

questions.question: What is abstraction? Abstract class vs interface?
questions.answer: Abstraction hides implementation details and exposes only functionality. Abstract class: can have both abstract and concrete methods, fields, constructors; single inheritance. Interface: only abstract methods + constants historically; since Java 8 also default/static methods; Java 9 also private methods; allows multiple inheritance.

questions.question: Can we declare a class private or protected?
questions.answer: A top-level class can only be public or package-private (no modifier). Inner/nested classes can be private or protected.

questions.question: What is a constructor?
questions.answer: A special block with the same name as the class, called when an object is created. It initializes the object's state. If none is defined, the compiler provides a no-arg default constructor.

questions.question: Difference between default, parameterized, and copy constructor?
questions.answer: Default: no args; compiler-generated if none defined. Parameterized: accepts args to initialize fields. Copy: takes another instance of the same class to duplicate its state (Java doesn't provide one automatically; you write it yourself).

questions.question: Can a constructor be final, static, or abstract?
questions.answer: No. Constructors aren't inherited (so final is meaningless), they operate on instances (so static is wrong), and they need a body (so abstract is wrong).

questions.question: What is the this keyword?
questions.answer: A reference to the current object. Used to refer to instance fields, invoke other constructors via this(...), or pass the current object to a method.

questions.question: What is the super keyword?
questions.answer: A reference to the immediate parent class. Used to call parent methods (super.method()), access parent fields, or invoke the parent constructor (super(...)).

questions.question: What is the static keyword?
questions.answer: Marks a field/method/block/nested class as belonging to the class itself, not to instances. Shared across all objects. Loaded once when the class is loaded.

questions.question: Can we override a static method?
questions.answer: No. Static methods belong to the class, not instances. They are hidden in subclasses, not overridden. Polymorphism doesn't apply.

questions.question: Why are Strings immutable in Java?
questions.answer: Three reasons: security (safe for passwords, class loaders, URLs), thread safety (no synchronization needed), and String Pool optimization (same literal shared across references). Bonus: hashCode can be cached.

questions.question: What is the String Pool?
questions.answer: A special region in the heap where String literals are stored. Writing String s = "hello" twice makes both references point to the same pool object, saving memory.

questions.question: Difference between String, StringBuilder, and StringBuffer?
questions.answer: String is immutable. StringBuilder is mutable, not thread-safe, fast — prefer in single-threaded code. StringBuffer is mutable, thread-safe (synchronized), slower.

questions.question: Difference between String s = "abc" and String s = new String("abc")?
questions.answer: The literal form uses/reuses the String Pool (one object). The new form creates a new object in the heap in addition to the pool entry — so potentially two objects exist.

questions.question: How do you compare two strings in Java?
questions.answer: Use .equals() for content comparison, .equalsIgnoreCase() for case-insensitive, and .compareTo() for lexicographic order. Never use == unless you genuinely want reference equality.

questions.question: What does intern() do?
questions.answer: Returns the canonical representation of the string from the pool. If the string is already pooled, returns the pooled reference; otherwise, adds it to the pool and returns the reference.

questions.question: Is String a primitive or an object in Java?
questions.answer: An object — java.lang.String. Java has no primitive string type.

questions.question: Can we use String in a switch statement?
questions.answer: Yes, since Java 7. Before that, switch only supported primitives and enums.

questions.question: What is the output of "10" + 20 + 30?
questions.answer: "102030". Evaluation is left-to-right: "10"+20 → "1020" (String concat), then "1020"+30 → "102030".

questions.question: How do you reverse a string in Java?
questions.answer: Easiest: new StringBuilder(s).reverse().toString(). Or iterate from end to start manually (common coding question).

questions.question: What is the Java Collections Framework?
questions.answer: A unified architecture for storing and manipulating groups of objects. Core interfaces: Collection, List, Set, Queue, Map (Map doesn't extend Collection, by design).

questions.question: Why doesn't Map extend Collection?
questions.answer: Because a Map is a key-value pair structure, not a single-element collection. The Collection contract (add(E), remove(E)) doesn't fit naturally.

questions.question: Difference between ArrayList and LinkedList?
questions.answer: ArrayList: backed by a dynamic array; O(1) random access via index; slower mid-list insertions (shifts). LinkedList: doubly-linked list; O(1) insertions/deletions at head/tail; O(n) random access; higher memory overhead per node.

questions.question: Difference between ArrayList and Vector?
questions.answer: Vector is synchronized (thread-safe but slower) and legacy; ArrayList is not synchronized (fast; use Collections.synchronizedList or CopyOnWriteArrayList if concurrency is needed). Vector doubles on resize; ArrayList grows by ~50%.

questions.question: Difference between HashMap and Hashtable?
questions.answer: HashMap: not synchronized, allows one null key and many null values, fast. Hashtable: synchronized, no null keys/values, legacy. Prefer ConcurrentHashMap over Hashtable for concurrent use.

questions.question: How does HashMap work internally?
questions.answer: An array of buckets (default 16, power of two). hashCode() of the key determines the bucket index. On collision, entries are stored in a linked list — or since Java 8, a red-black tree once a bucket exceeds 8 entries. equals() resolves which exact key matches.

questions.question: What is the load factor of a HashMap?
questions.answer: The threshold (default 0.75) that triggers rehashing. When the map is 75% full, the internal array doubles. Balances space vs time.

questions.question: What happens if two keys have the same hashCode?
questions.answer: Collision. Both entries land in the same bucket, stored as a linked list (or red-black tree since Java 8). On lookup, equals() distinguishes the exact key.

questions.question: What contract must hashCode() and equals() satisfy?
questions.answer: If a.equals(b) is true, then a.hashCode() == b.hashCode() must be true. Equal hash codes don't imply equals. equals must be reflexive, symmetric, transitive, and consistent. Breaking this breaks every hash-based collection.

questions.question: Difference between HashMap, LinkedHashMap, and TreeMap?
questions.answer: HashMap: no order, O(1) average, allows one null key. LinkedHashMap: maintains insertion (or access) order, O(1) average. TreeMap: sorted by keys (natural or Comparator), O(log n), no null keys.

questions.question: Difference between HashSet and TreeSet?
questions.answer: HashSet: unordered, O(1) average, backed by HashMap, allows one null. TreeSet: sorted, O(log n), backed by TreeMap, implements NavigableSet, doesn't allow null.

questions.question: Difference between Iterator and ListIterator?
questions.answer: Iterator: forward-only, works on any Collection. ListIterator: bidirectional, works only on Lists, supports add() and set() during iteration.

questions.question: Fail-fast vs fail-safe iterators?
questions.answer: Fail-fast throws ConcurrentModificationException if the collection is modified during iteration (e.g., ArrayList, HashMap iterators). Fail-safe iterates over a copy, no exception, may miss latest changes (e.g., ConcurrentHashMap, CopyOnWriteArrayList).

questions.question: Difference between Comparable and Comparator?
questions.answer: Comparable: natural ordering, implemented by the class itself via compareTo(), single sort order. Comparator: external, separate class or lambda via compare(), multiple sort orders possible.

questions.question: How does ConcurrentHashMap work?
questions.answer: Java 7 used segment-level locking (lock stripes). Java 8+ uses fine-grained synchronization at the bucket level with CAS and a synchronized lock on the first node — far better concurrency than Hashtable's full-map lock.

questions.question: What is CopyOnWriteArrayList?
questions.answer: A thread-safe List variant. Every write (add/set/remove) creates a fresh copy of the underlying array. Great for read-heavy, rarely-written scenarios — terrible if writes are frequent.

questions.question: What is BlockingQueue?
questions.answer: A Queue that blocks on put() when full and take() when empty — the foundation of producer-consumer patterns. Implementations: ArrayBlockingQueue, LinkedBlockingQueue, PriorityBlockingQueue.

questions.question: What is PriorityQueue?
questions.answer: A Queue where elements are ordered by priority (natural or Comparator). The head is always the minimum element. O(log n) insert/remove.

questions.question: Can you store primitive types in collections?
questions.answer: No. Collections only hold objects. Primitives are autoboxed into wrapper classes (int → Integer, etc.) when added.

questions.question: How do you make a collection read-only?
questions.answer: Collections.unmodifiableList(list) / unmodifiableMap / unmodifiableSet. Since Java 9, also List.of(...), Map.of(...), Set.of(...) for truly immutable instances.

questions.question: What is an exception?
questions.answer: An abnormal event that disrupts normal program flow. Java represents them as objects of Throwable or its subclasses.

questions.question: Hierarchy of exceptions in Java?
questions.answer: Throwable splits into Error (unrecoverable, e.g., OutOfMemoryError) and Exception (recoverable). Exception splits further into checked exceptions and unchecked (RuntimeException and its subclasses).

questions.question: Checked vs unchecked exceptions?
questions.answer: Checked: verified at compile time; must be declared or caught (e.g., IOException, SQLException). Unchecked: verified at runtime; no compile-time enforcement (e.g., NullPointerException, ArithmeticException).

questions.question: Difference between throw and throws?
questions.answer: throw: actually throws an exception instance (throw new IOException()). throws: method signature declaration of what a method may throw.

questions.question: What does finally do? When does it NOT execute?
questions.answer: finally always runs after try/catch for cleanup. It won't execute if the JVM exits (System.exit()), the thread is killed, or an infinite loop/deadlock occurs in try.

questions.question: Can we have try without catch?
questions.answer: Yes. try-finally is valid. Also try-with-resources (Java 7+) doesn't need an explicit catch.

questions.question: What is try-with-resources?
questions.answer: A syntax (Java 7+) that auto-closes any resource implementing AutoCloseable — no explicit finally block needed. Cleaner and safer than manual close.

questions.question: Can we catch multiple exceptions in one catch block?
questions.answer: Yes, since Java 7: catch (IOException | SQLException e). The caught variable is implicitly final.

questions.question: Can a finally block override a return from try?
questions.answer: Yes — if finally has its own return, it overrides try's return. Considered bad practice; static analyzers warn against it.

questions.question: How do you create a custom exception?
questions.answer: Extend Exception (checked) or RuntimeException (unchecked), add constructors that delegate to super. Use a meaningful name like InsufficientBalanceException.

questions.question: Process vs Thread?
questions.answer: A process is an independent program with its own memory space. A thread is a lightweight unit of execution inside a process, sharing the heap but with its own stack.

questions.question: How do you create a thread in Java?
questions.answer: Two ways: extend Thread and override run(), or implement Runnable and pass it to a Thread constructor. Option 2 is preferred — keeps inheritance free and works with lambdas/executors.

questions.question: Difference between start() and run()?
questions.answer: start() creates a new thread and calls run() on it. Calling run() directly just executes the code on the current thread — no concurrency.

questions.question: What are the thread states?
questions.answer: NEW → RUNNABLE → (RUNNING) → BLOCKED / WAITING / TIMED_WAITING → TERMINATED.

questions.question: What is the synchronized keyword?
questions.answer: Locks either an instance (synchronized method), a class (synchronized static method), or an object (synchronized(lock) block). Ensures only one thread at a time executes the protected section.

questions.question: What is the volatile keyword?
questions.answer: Guarantees visibility: a write by one thread is immediately visible to others; prevents caching in registers. Does NOT guarantee atomicity — use AtomicInteger etc. for that.

questions.question: Difference between wait(), sleep(), and yield()?
questions.answer: wait(): releases the lock, waits on an object's monitor (must be inside synchronized block); called on an object. sleep(): pauses the thread for given time; does NOT release locks; called on Thread. yield(): hint to the scheduler that this thread is willing to pause; no guaranteed effect.

questions.question: What is a deadlock?
questions.answer: Two or more threads waiting forever for locks held by each other, forming a cycle. Prevention: fixed lock ordering, timeouts (tryLock), avoiding nested locks.

questions.question: Race condition vs Livelock vs Starvation?
questions.answer: Race condition: multiple threads access shared state without proper sync, leading to unpredictable results. Livelock: threads are running but keep reacting to each other with no progress. Starvation: a thread never gets CPU/resources because higher-priority threads monopolize them.

questions.question: What is the Executor / ExecutorService framework?
questions.answer: A higher-level thread management abstraction introduced in Java 5. Instead of creating threads manually, submit tasks to a thread pool (Executors.newFixedThreadPool(...)) that reuses threads.

questions.question: Difference between Runnable and Callable?
questions.answer: Runnable: run() returns void; can't throw checked exceptions. Callable<V>: call() returns a value and can throw checked exceptions; paired with Future.

questions.question: What is Future and CompletableFuture?
questions.answer: Future: placeholder for an async result; blocking get(). CompletableFuture (Java 8): non-blocking, composable pipelines via thenApply, thenCompose, thenCombine, with async callbacks.

questions.question: What is the Java Memory Model (JMM)?
questions.answer: Defines how threads interact through memory: what writes are visible to what reads, rules around volatile, synchronized, and final fields, and the 'happens-before' relationship.

questions.question: Difference between CyclicBarrier and CountDownLatch?
questions.answer: CountDownLatch: one-shot; threads wait until a count reaches zero; not reusable. CyclicBarrier: threads wait at a barrier until all arrive; reusable after tripping.

questions.question: What are atomic classes like AtomicInteger?
questions.answer: Lock-free thread-safe classes in java.util.concurrent.atomic that use CAS (Compare-And-Swap) for atomic updates without synchronization overhead.

questions.question: What are the key features introduced in Java 8?
questions.answer: Lambda expressions, Stream API, Functional Interfaces, Method References, default/static methods in interfaces, Optional, new Date/Time API (java.time), CompletableFuture, Nashorn JS engine.

questions.question: What is a lambda expression?
questions.answer: A concise, anonymous function — a block of code you can pass around like data. Syntax: (params) -> expression or (params) -> { statements; }. Can only be assigned to a functional interface.

questions.question: What is a functional interface?
questions.answer: An interface with exactly one abstract method (default/static methods don't count). Examples: Runnable, Comparator, Predicate, Function, Consumer, Supplier. Use @FunctionalInterface for compiler enforcement.

questions.question: What is the Stream API?
questions.answer: An abstraction for processing sequences of elements declaratively — filter, map, reduce, collect. Streams are lazy, consume a source (collection/array), and support parallel execution.

questions.question: Intermediate vs terminal operations on a Stream?
questions.answer: Intermediate: return a new stream, lazy (filter, map, sorted, distinct). Terminal: trigger execution and produce a result (collect, forEach, reduce, count).

questions.question: Difference between map() and flatMap()?
questions.answer: map applies a function to each element (1-to-1 transform). flatMap applies a function that returns a stream, then flattens the result (1-to-many, flattened into one stream).

questions.question: What is Optional?
questions.answer: A container for a possibly-null value. Helps avoid NullPointerException by forcing explicit absence handling: isPresent(), orElse(), map(), ifPresent(). Use for return values, not fields/parameters.

questions.question: What is a method reference?
questions.answer: Shorthand for a lambda that just calls a method: String::toUpperCase instead of s -> s.toUpperCase(). Four kinds: static, bound instance, unbound instance, constructor reference.

questions.question: What are default methods in interfaces?
questions.answer: Methods with a body inside an interface (Java 8+), marked with default. They let you add new methods to interfaces without breaking existing implementations — how List.forEach was introduced.

questions.question: Difference between Collection and Stream?
questions.answer: A Collection stores data in memory. A Stream is a computation pipeline over data — it doesn't store, it processes. Streams are lazy, single-use, and can be parallelized.

questions.question: What is serialization?
questions.answer: Converting an object into a byte stream for storage or transmission; deserialization reverses it. Class must implement Serializable (marker). Use transient to skip fields. serialVersionUID handles version compatibility.

questions.question: Shallow copy vs deep copy?
questions.answer: Shallow: copies object fields as-is; nested object references are shared. Deep: recursively copies nested objects too; fully independent duplicate.

questions.question: What are generics and type erasure?
questions.answer: Generics provide compile-time type safety (List<String>). Type erasure: at runtime, generic info is stripped (List<String> → List) for backward compatibility with pre-generics code.

questions.question: What is garbage collection? Can you force it?
questions.answer: Automatic memory reclamation of unreachable objects. You can suggest it via System.gc() or Runtime.getRuntime().gc(), but the JVM decides. Algorithms: Serial, Parallel, G1 (default since Java 9), ZGC, Shenandoah.

questions.question: Difference between ==, equals(), and hashCode() for objects?
questions.answer: == checks reference identity. equals() checks logical equality (override for value-based classes). hashCode() returns an int used by hash-based collections. Rule: if you override equals, you MUST override hashCode consistently.

study_plan.freshers_0_to_2_yrs: Focus on Q1–Q70 (Basics, OOP, Strings, Collections, Exceptions). All MOST_FREQ in these sections.
study_plan.mid_level_2_to_5_yrs: Add Q71–Q95 (Multithreading, Java 8). Dig deeper into Q46 (HashMap internals), Q55 (ConcurrentHashMap), Q83 (JMM).
study_plan.senior_5_plus_yrs: All 100 + follow-ups on JVM internals, GC tuning, and real-world concurrency bugs.
